speaker: interviewer
text: The-- it's like a-- massive--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I took it a little more in step at the time. You just were kind of shocked, but couldn't stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm putting some bars in front of the tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want me to slate it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, just say tape 2.

speaker: interviewer
text: Second tape?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. When I tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: With Walter Mason.

speaker: interviewer
text: I apologize. Just one more time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 2 with Walter Mason.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Walter, sharing about-- you're in the barracks. You see them with the pans. Please go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were extremely emaciated, and obviously malnutrition. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was general throughout the camp. There were many, many real thin ones. There were some who looked healthy, and apparently they had been favored with some duties by the officers of the camp. But most of the general prisoners were-- average weight was less than 90 pounds. The sanitation in the camp was very poor. Trenches were the means of getting rid of some of the waste matter to the human excrement and so on. Dirt was prevalent. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] generally it was a deplorable, [BACKROUND NOISE] messy condition. It's hard to describe in, in detail.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your immediate reaction at that moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess anger. How anybody could treat another human being like that or allow it to happen was difficult to understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any of the prisoners who were coming up to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As matter of fact, we did put on our Red Cross insignia, and a couple of them came up. One of them got a bunch of flowers and handed them to me and doffed his cap like a welcome. But I wasn't able to communicate with very many of them. Not any of them that I saw spoke much English. We did use sign language, and they would take us from one place to another to show us where they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were wearing these striped pajama-type uniforms, and most-- some of them had stars on them, the Star of David. And others had no insignia but numbers. And most of them had shaved heads. Some of them had beards but not long beards, just stubble growth on them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there women and men?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw no women where I went. Whether there were women there, I, I was not aware, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your commanders at that time telling you to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most of them were, at that time, organizing details to clean out the SS barracks, which were nearby. They had some rather, well, sturdy barracks that they planned on using as a hospital routine. And the order was to transfer those that were living into those quarters where they could receive some nutritional support and medical treatment that we might be able to provide at that time. I did not participate in that particular group. The chief surgeon was involved a little bit in contacting the doctors who were in their own hospital there, many of whom were prisoners themselves. And it was my understanding they had done some experimentation on typhus. And they were looking at some of the patients in the, in the hospital itself, and those were the really bad ones, the open wounds. And I can remember yet seeing the legs wrapped in what looked like, to me, toilet tissue. And that was their traditional wrapping of a wound. He got into a discussion with one of the doctors there, and they had never heard of penicillin. And of course, that was new to us. So he asked me if I would go with him, and they'd give a demonstration on the use of penicillin and what it would do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a limited amount we were able to give to them. And at that time, penicillin didn't come in the little vials like we see them now. We had soluble tablets that we had to mix up just prior to the administration of the penicillin and-- so I would show them how we'd use normal saline or sterile water and put the tablet in the vial and mix up the appropriate strength for the injections. You're referring to the prisoners who were also doctors at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the prisoners were taking care of-- yes, they were also doctors, but they were taking care of the, the hospitalized prisoners. In my estimation, all of them should have been in a hospital, but the ones that were surgically required to be in a hospital where there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some of the more severe cases were hospitalized. Why they-- how they selected who was to go to a hospital and who was not, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would be in charge of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They left that in charge of the doctors in that, in that particular-- we were usually, we were used primarily as advisors and what they might be able to accomplish with what they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish soldiers in your unit-- what, if any, difference was their reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never was aware of any big reaction. I found out later that some of them who did speak Yiddish did get together with some of the prisoners, and they begin to exchange some of the stories. These didn't come out until well after we'd left the camp. But they had talked about what transpired prior to their commitment to the concentration camp and some of the hardships that they had to endure during the camp stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any of these areas where you said they were doing some experiments with typhus medication?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Pretty much of that had been cleared out. We did see the infamous lampshades made of human skin that were collected by the camp commander and his wife. His wife was particularly fond of collecting tattoos. And there were tattoos that were mounted on plaques. And when they got those from the prisoners, I don't know. Whether they had them killed if the tattoo was attractive or not, I'm not sure to say, but I did view that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- were you aware of what it was when you first saw it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were told, yes. I was a aware of what it was. There again, it was anger, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it had to be controlled.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the name of the camp commander who had been there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back a few moments to entering the gates of Buchenwald. As you walked through, who led?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just walked through. There was no, no organizational deal. We came in in groups, small groups, as we were available. I remember looking at the sign above the main gate, and as I recall, it read something like, [GERMAN], which I was told was interpreted, right or wrong, my father-- my country or my fatherland. And I thought, how can they be right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any German soldiers present?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we captured three German soldiers. I shouldn't say captured. They came in to give themselves up to us. I suspect they saw the red crosses and figured that would be a good time to-- good unit to give up to. We just kept them in detention until the military police could come and pick them up. But I think there were three German soldiers. The rest of them had fled before we had arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you offer food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were told not to because they-- their condition was such that if they were given any rich food-- like we had a candy bar or a C-ration or something like that-- their system just couldn't tolerate it. The first thing that they did was give intravenous fluids to build up their nutritional system so they could take solid food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the prisoners asking for food at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No one was begging for food. They just looked wide-eyed and stared at you. It was an empty, empty, glassy stare.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the majority of the living prisoners when you walked in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them were in the, in the barracks. We saw a few of them, I found out later, that were working on binoculars, repairing them. They looked healthier. I suspect if they had some skills they were kept working. But most of them that we saw that were still there were just laying in these trays of human beings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of the men react in a, a, a sick fashion from this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't say that they did. I, I think, at the time, there was a determination to do what you had to do. And I don't know whether it's adrenaline that takes over or what, but nobody was doing anything but the appropriate thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you write anything down, any notes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't at that time. I, I did take a number of pictures because I wanted to document this for people whom I'm sure wouldn't believe it if they were told the visual description of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll show that at the end of our taping. In the meantime, were there smells when you walked in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. This-- you got used to it after a couple days, but the smells were indescribable. They-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one of the fellows described it as just the smell of death. There were, of course, lots of diarrhea, human fecal material around, but there was another odd, almost indescribable odor to the whole place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you write letters home about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only wrote one to my brother describing the things because I didn't feel that I was adequate to talk to anybody else who would understand it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see where they prepared a food form that they had?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a view of the kitchen, and all I could see was the bread that they were baking. They did have bread there, and they were in the process of distributing some pieces of bread to the various prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the prisoners able to be fed, or did they all have to go on intravenous?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most of them were able to feed-- be fed-- but a good share of them couldn't take any solid food until after intravenous products were given. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the, the locale around the camp? What does it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the camp was on a hill, and it was a beautiful sight. Weimar, as I understood it, was a cultural city. There was the Duke of Weimar-- had a castle up there. Actually, we stayed in a huge area there. They had a school, as I understand it, next to it, and we stayed in some of those sections. And some of the officers stayed in the more luxurious area. And it was there that we entertained the members of Congress and some of the members of Parliament who were invited to come over to see firsthand what we had uncovered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the, any of the people who did come?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I saw them, but we were not allowed to-- I was just a sergeant. The officers were doing the entertaining of the big wheels.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the names of the people who came?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. The only one that I remember that I knew was Margaret Bourke-White, who I recognized. I was interested in photography, and when I saw her up there taking pictures, I knew that this was going to be a national project. I mean, it was going to get national exposure.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you were to look back on an overview of the camp, what did you see? The buildings, the type of buildings. What was that--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was dark, a grayish, non-colorful setting. Everything looked depressing. There were no trees that I can remember, no walkways, no flowers. Matter of fact, when the one patient brought flowers, I couldn't imagine where in the world he could've gotten them from. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Just everything stripped to its barest. And even the uniforms were a black and white or an off-white and dark colors. Everything was pale and black.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember seeing any crematoriums?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did view the crematoriums. And the ashes and some of the skeletons were still in there. It was my understanding that they had received orders prior to the advancement of the Allied forces that they should destroy the prisoners that were sick and ill. But obviously, they never had the time because there must have been a couple hundred bodies still in the courtyard there by the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever hear of any talk that they had wanted to destroy the whole camp before you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never any specific ones, but-- talk about destroying the camp itself, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find out if there was anybody of prominence in the communities from Europe that were in those-- in Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was-- I should say, Elie Wiesel, I found out later was one of the prisoners, and he, of course, had won the Peace Prize at a later date. But I wasn't aware of that until many, many years after the fact.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you set up your facilities there, what more did you start to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's about all we did do. That's about all we had time to do. We continued separating the dead from the living and taking care of those that could be, could receive some nutritional and medical support. And then we were ordered on, and we left our duties to another unit that came in shortly after we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the other unit that came through?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I do not. Part of the group that took over were international in scope. The Red Cross particularly came in, and they began process of repatriation to those that were able to do so.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: About two weeks. It was a fairly short period of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember many dying during the time you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, our record of saving was pretty good. I saved an article from which kind of epitomized the work that we did there. And if I, if I may, I'd like to read that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the date of the article?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is from May 14, 1945. It was under the headline of 'medicine,' and I had cut this out and saved it with my unit history book. And it's entitled 'Back from the Grave.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And it says, 'After the first, sickening blows of horror brought the news of the German concentration camps came the question, how sick are the human remnants left alive? From Buchenwald last week, correspondent Percy Knauth reported how the US Army doctors have tackled the job.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the 120th Army Evacuation Hospital, commanded by William E.-- Colonel William E. Williams of Austin, Texas, has fallen the terrible task of trying to save the lives of the Buchenwald sick and starving. The hospital moved into Buchenwald April 16th and since then has been working at top speed, helped by French, German, Czech, Hungarian, and other doctors among the inmates. There were 2,180 sick in the camp hospital alone when the hospital unit arrived. The first evening, they got 161 more from the barracks in the so-called small camp, the worst section of Buchenwald, where the dying were sent when they were considered beyond human care. Most of these patients were suffering from diarrhea caused simply by lack of food. In the small camp, men were still dying by the hundreds. The 120th took over two of the former SS barracks, cleaned them out with help of internees, and they put in cots, blankets, medical supplies, and their own-- of their own issue. A typhus ward was organized in a building formally used by SS doctors for typhus experiments on the prisoners. Then the Army and inmate doctors readied 15 other SS buildings in the camp and started to clean out the terrible barracks of the small camp, in which the starving men lay packed by rotting-- packed like rotting cargo on bare, wooden shelves reaching from the floor to the ceiling. I saw these barracks a few days ago, and it was all I could do to stand the sight and smell of the parodies of human beings who, who inhabited them. They were alive by instinct only, and by instinct they were-- almost knocked me down when I produced from my pocket one pitiful chocolate bar in answer to a plea of a prisoner for food. At the sight of that morsel of food, these filthy, spidery human beings were galvanized by a single impulse, to get a crumb, just a crumb. It was something to put in their stomachs. I saw the barracks again today-- empty, washed, and with a clean wind blowing through them but still permeated by that awful smell of death. I saw skeletons of men walking and creeping in a dazed way up and down the halls, unable yet to understand what-- that they were really being fed and nursed back to life. Everything that medical skill could give them, they had received. They were first washed in sit-down bathtubs, and then clean and fresh clothes. They were put to bed in sunlit rooms with flowers. Whole blood was transfused into emaciated bodies. Nourishment was injected intravenously-- nourishment was injected intravenously when they could not digest strengthening soups, but more than anything else, the simple fact that humane care and decent surroundings had its effect. In the first two days after the 120th moved in, only two men died.' [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I think that kind of summarized our activities there. What were you aware of the German people around the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well at first, I couldn't believe that they knew about that. And they claimed innocence of that. But I couldn't see how they could see the smoke from the chimneys and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] see the activity around the camp and not know, to some extent, how they could be ignorant of that fact. But I think there had developed at that time a 'I think I know what's going on, but I don't want to know' attitude that perhaps we sometimes are guilty of here in the United States as well. We don't want to know and don't take the opportunity to find out. But the commander, General Patton, [BACKROUND NOISE] had ordered that the quartermaster corps take trucks down and bring these people up there and show them the atrocities as we found them. And some of those pictures that I showed you indicate the-- I tried to catch the reaction of some of the people as they saw that, and looking at the bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe their reactions?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were horrified, I believe, genuinely horrified. Whether they were horrified at trying to find out that what they thought was true was true, or whether this was a total surprise, I wouldn't want to make that judgement.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any interaction between your unit and the German people of the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had no interaction between them at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn more about the camp later on as you-- after you left, and what did you learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was probably more after I'd left the service that I learned about that. We had a tendency to put it behind us and go on to what our next task was. I, I probably became more aware of the general condition that existed when we reached our next assignment in Kamm, Germany, where we treated displaced persons from several parts of Europe, particularly near the Czechoslovakian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the facilities that you went to next.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were aware that a number of people were, toward the end of the war, were running around with diseases and were needing treatment. The Army thought that we ought to set up some sort of hospitalization facilities in this particular area, so we went into what was a pretty, little town of Kamm, Germany, relatively untouched by the ravages of war. And we were able to take over-- actually, I think we had four hotels in the group. I was particularly aware of one where we set up our pharmacy in the lobby of the [NON-ENGLISH] Hotel. And there we would take in patients who were wandering throughout the fields. Sometimes they were brought in by Red Cross, or-- I don't know who actually found them. But they heard about our location there and brought them in. I didn't know how many patients there were until I read a unit history, and I found out that we had somewhere in excess of 900 patients throughout those four hospitals. And we tried to give them the basic medical needs. Most of it was diarrhea, lung conditions, and severe coughing and malnutrition were the primary reasons they couldn't move and couldn't get back to their homeland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you interact, or how would the unit interact with these patients with languages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly by sign language. I think, probably, the most dramatic one I can remember how we adapted to the sign language figure. We had one-- I think he was a French doctor-- came in and was asking for some medical supplies, and we were unable to pass the barrier to determine just exactly what he needed until he began to hold his stomach and he went-- And we determined that he was in need of something to control the diarrhea that was prevalent in the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your supply of medical needs?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in reasonably good supply. I don't remember where we got restocked from, but we had in our stock, in our basic stock, a 55-gallon drum of pure grain alcohol, from which we made our own paregoric and bismuth combinations to treat the diarrhea. And we also made our own terpin hydrate and codine to act as an expectorant and cough sedative for those who had severe lung conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a-- we were able to make gallons and gallons of that from our initial stock that we had never touched. Our unit had never really taken any-- from the time we hit Europe, we never took any American [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] patients per se. It turned out that we ended up treating concentration camp internees and displaced persons primarily. You mentioned there were over 900 patients in these hospitals. How many-- or what percentage would you say were the Jewish prisoners in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I never was pertinent to that information. You could tell by looking at their characteristics that many of them may have been Jewish, but it was difficult to tell whether they were Jewish or Bulgarian. I'm not that much of a racial [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] expert on facial characteristics. We didn't ask, and they didn't tell us. Some of them we knew were Jewish or had been in concentration camps because we could see the tattoos of the numbers on their arms or something of that nature.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe an average day for you in these hospitals.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the other-- the senior pharmacist and myself would concentrate just on filling the needs of the wards. The wards would have some basic needs like cough syrups and antacids and [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] the blood they got from another source. We didn't handle the blood, but they would come down and get some of the basic pain relievers and expectorants and things of that nature. And then if there were some specific needs, then we'd see if our stock would fulfill that, and we'd send that medication on up to the wards. But I never came in contact with the patients themselves. The nurses did most of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would they share information with you about the patients?

speaker: interviewee
text: We never talked a whole lot about it at the time, except to make general comments about how generally poor their condition had been and how encouraged they were when they saw progress.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever receive any psychological help about what you had been experiencing in--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, we never did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel you had a need at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't think so. You reflect upon it, but I think you take it in, in its context and store it away. I think a lot of it we push behind us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop.
